rationale: In the theory section, the authors explicitly cite Roehm and Tybout (2006) for the claim that spillover is more likely when the focal brand is a typical category member. They use that logic to derive a central hypothesis (H3) about asymmetric spillover from dominant to less dominant brands, so the citation supports hypothesis development rather than serving as a passing background mention. The citing paper then tests this idea in a new empirical setting (automobile recalls and social media chatter), indicating a meaningful build-on relationship.
relation_description: The citing paper extends Roehm and Tybout (2006) from experimental brand-scandal spillover to recall-driven spillover in social media and market outcomes. It adopts their typicality-based spillover logic and operationalizes it via brand dominance (market share) to test directional asymmetry.
